frames.caption: Root is not null, so enqueue it. The queue now holds level 0: just [5].
frames.note: BFS needs a seed. We push the root and then process the tree in waves: the queue always contains exactly one full level at a time, which is what lets us group the output by depth.

frames.caption: Start level 0: snapshot queue.Count = 1. The queue holds exactly this level's 1 node.
frames.note: Here is the trick that turns a flat BFS into a level-by-level one: the moment we are about to drain a level, the queue holds EXACTLY the nodes of that level and nothing else. So we snapshot queue.Count into levelSize and dequeue precisely that many nodes. Every child we enqueue belongs to the NEXT level and is safely past the snapshot, so it cannot contaminate this level.

frames.caption: Dequeue 5 and append it to level 0. level = [5].
frames.note: Dequeue from the FRONT (FIFO) — the oldest node still waiting. We append its value to the current level list. Because the queue drains a whole level before any of the children we are about to add, BFS visits top-to-bottom, left-to-right, which is exactly the level-order shape LC 102 asks for.

frames.caption: 5 has a child 3 → enqueue it for the next level. Queue: [3].
frames.note: Each non-null child goes to the BACK of the queue. It belongs to the next level down, so it must wait until every node of the current level is processed first — that waiting is precisely what the FIFO queue enforces for free.

frames.caption: 5 has a child 8 → enqueue it for the next level. Queue: [3, 8].
frames.note: Each non-null child goes to the BACK of the queue. It belongs to the next level down, so it must wait until every node of the current level is processed first — that waiting is precisely what the FIFO queue enforces for free.

frames.caption: Level 0 complete → commit [5] to result. result = [[5]].
frames.note: The inner loop ran levelSize times, so we have collected every node on this level in left-to-right order. We commit that list to the result and loop back: the queue now holds exactly the next level, and the snapshot trick repeats.

frames.caption: Start level 1: snapshot queue.Count = 2. The queue holds exactly this level's 2 nodes.
frames.note: Here is the trick that turns a flat BFS into a level-by-level one: the moment we are about to drain a level, the queue holds EXACTLY the nodes of that level and nothing else. So we snapshot queue.Count into levelSize and dequeue precisely that many nodes. Every child we enqueue belongs to the NEXT level and is safely past the snapshot, so it cannot contaminate this level.

frames.caption: Dequeue 3 and append it to level 1. level = [3].
frames.note: Dequeue from the FRONT (FIFO) — the oldest node still waiting. We append its value to the current level list. Because the queue drains a whole level before any of the children we are about to add, BFS visits top-to-bottom, left-to-right, which is exactly the level-order shape LC 102 asks for.

frames.caption: 3 has a child 1 → enqueue it for the next level. Queue: [8, 1].
frames.note: Each non-null child goes to the BACK of the queue. It belongs to the next level down, so it must wait until every node of the current level is processed first — that waiting is precisely what the FIFO queue enforces for free.

frames.caption: 3 has a child 4 → enqueue it for the next level. Queue: [8, 1, 4].
frames.note: Each non-null child goes to the BACK of the queue. It belongs to the next level down, so it must wait until every node of the current level is processed first — that waiting is precisely what the FIFO queue enforces for free.

frames.caption: Dequeue 8 and append it to level 1. level = [3, 8].
frames.note: Dequeue from the FRONT (FIFO) — the oldest node still waiting. We append its value to the current level list. Because the queue drains a whole level before any of the children we are about to add, BFS visits top-to-bottom, left-to-right, which is exactly the level-order shape LC 102 asks for.

frames.caption: 8 has a child 9 → enqueue it for the next level. Queue: [1, 4, 9].
frames.note: Each non-null child goes to the BACK of the queue. It belongs to the next level down, so it must wait until every node of the current level is processed first — that waiting is precisely what the FIFO queue enforces for free.

frames.caption: Level 1 complete → commit [3, 8] to result. result = [[5], [3, 8]].
frames.note: The inner loop ran levelSize times, so we have collected every node on this level in left-to-right order. We commit that list to the result and loop back: the queue now holds exactly the next level, and the snapshot trick repeats.

frames.caption: Start level 2: snapshot queue.Count = 3. The queue holds exactly this level's 3 nodes.
frames.note: Here is the trick that turns a flat BFS into a level-by-level one: the moment we are about to drain a level, the queue holds EXACTLY the nodes of that level and nothing else. So we snapshot queue.Count into levelSize and dequeue precisely that many nodes. Every child we enqueue belongs to the NEXT level and is safely past the snapshot, so it cannot contaminate this level.

frames.caption: Dequeue 1 and append it to level 2. level = [1].
frames.note: Dequeue from the FRONT (FIFO) — the oldest node still waiting. We append its value to the current level list. Because the queue drains a whole level before any of the children we are about to add, BFS visits top-to-bottom, left-to-right, which is exactly the level-order shape LC 102 asks for.

frames.caption: 1 is a leaf — no children to enqueue.
frames.note: A leaf adds nothing to the frontier. The queue simply shrinks by one. When it finally drains to empty, BFS is done.

frames.caption: Dequeue 4 and append it to level 2. level = [1, 4].
frames.note: Dequeue from the FRONT (FIFO) — the oldest node still waiting. We append its value to the current level list. Because the queue drains a whole level before any of the children we are about to add, BFS visits top-to-bottom, left-to-right, which is exactly the level-order shape LC 102 asks for.

frames.caption: 4 is a leaf — no children to enqueue.
frames.note: A leaf adds nothing to the frontier. The queue simply shrinks by one. When it finally drains to empty, BFS is done.

frames.caption: Dequeue 9 and append it to level 2. level = [1, 4, 9].
frames.note: Dequeue from the FRONT (FIFO) — the oldest node still waiting. We append its value to the current level list. Because the queue drains a whole level before any of the children we are about to add, BFS visits top-to-bottom, left-to-right, which is exactly the level-order shape LC 102 asks for.

frames.caption: 9 is a leaf — no children to enqueue.
frames.note: A leaf adds nothing to the frontier. The queue simply shrinks by one. When it finally drains to empty, BFS is done.

frames.caption: Level 2 complete → commit [1, 4, 9] to result. result = [[5], [3, 8], [1, 4, 9]].
frames.note: The inner loop ran levelSize times, so we have collected every node on this level in left-to-right order. We commit that list to the result and loop back: the queue now holds exactly the next level, and the snapshot trick repeats.

frames.caption: Queue is empty — every level has been emitted. Result = [[5], [3, 8], [1, 4, 9]].
frames.note: The queue drained level by level, so the result groups nodes by depth top-to-bottom. Time is O(n) (each node enqueued and dequeued once); space is O(w), the maximum level width — for this tree the widest level (1, 4, 9) holds 3 nodes. Reverse each odd level instead of appending and you get the LC 103 zigzag variant for free.
